turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: What was to follow or to continue, to carry on?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Seguir. And how was I continue or I carry on or I follow?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Sigo. Good. If you want to say I continue speaking, here we use the - ing, I continue speaking, so how would that be?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Sigo hablando. He continues speaking:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Sigue hablando. He continues speaking, she continues speaking, you (formal) continue speaking. It’s probable that he continues speaking he’ll probably. So, in English we will probably say continue speaking, but in Spanish it is probable that he continues speaking. So, how would that be?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Very good. Es probable que siga hablando. Good. It is probable that he continues speaking. So, after seguir to get the meaning of continue we can use the - ing. What was to be for characteristics?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Ser. Good. How would you make the - ing form of ser?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Siendo. Free, in the sense of not requiring any money, is gratis in Spanish.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: This is where we get gratuitous from in English. It’s a bit gratuitous, it’s unnecessary, it’s free, it’s it’s imperative that it continues to be free it’s imperative that it extra. Gratis. So, you can say. So, continues being free, how would you say that?

turns.speaker: student
expected_answers: Es imperativo que siga siendo gratis.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Es imperativo que siga siendo gratis. To start, we said that we had comenzar and empezar. Comenzar is, of course, easier to remember and use, because it sounds like commence, but empezar is probably more common. So, you can use whichever you like now. How would you it’s spectacular that they start to speak Spanish say? Now, after start, to connect to the next to form you need a. There are just a handful of verbs that are not verbs of movement, for example, like ir, volver, salir, that need a to connect, apart from these there are just a handful of verbs that use some preposition to connect, and empezar and comenzar are two of them. So, how would it’s spectacular that they begin to speak Spanish (spectacular you say is the same word in Spanish)?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Almost the same word.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good, es espectacular, that they begin:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Es espectacular que comienzen a hablar español. It's spectacular that they start to speak Spanish. Now, after comenzar and empezar you could also put the - ing form, and the meaning it’s spectacular that they start out speaking Spanish would change a little, it would mean. Now, in English there is not a lot of difference, that they begin to speak Spanish, that they begin speaking Spanish, both in English mean to start speaking Spanish. But in Spanish, if you use the - ing here it would mean that they start out speaking Spanish, like right from the beginning. So, when I’m speaking about these courses for example, I say that we start out speaking Spanish, right from the it’s spectacular that beginning. Now, speaking about that in Spanish I would use the - ing here. So, they start out speaking Spanish:

turns.speaker: student
expected_answers: Es espectacular que comienzen hablando español.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. And here we don’t need the a, because we are not connecting to a to form. What was the verb to finish or to terminate?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Terminar. Now, if you want to say a sentence like I want you guys to speak with me when you finish selling them, we will say when you finish to sell them. Here you won’t use the - ing, but to finish, terminar, is also one of those verbs that needs a preposition to connect to the next to form. In this case the preposition is de. So, to finish selling them is terminar de vender. So, I want you guys to speak with me when you finish selling them, so the first bit is I want you guys to speak with me, how is that bit?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good, mood tense, quiero que hablen conmigo. Good. I want that you talk with me, quiero que hablen conmigo, when you finish selling them. So, when you finish, and we are still speaking to you guys:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good, why did you use the mood tense there – cuando terminen?

turns.speaker: student
expected_answers: Because it’s the future when., we won’t use the mood tense, we just use the present,

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. When cuando refers to generally and here, referring to a future event that hasn’t happened, after cuando, we use the mood tense, well remembered. Quiero que hablen conmigo cuando terminen, when you finish selling them, and we are not going to use selling, we are going to use to sell, and we connect that with de:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good, so altogether – I want you guys to speak with me when you finish selling them:

turns.speaker: student
expected_answers: Quiero que hablen conmigo cuando terminen de venderlos.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Quiero que hablen conmigo cuando terminen de venderlos. So this means I want you guys to speak with me when you finish selling them, but terminar we can also use like end up: I want you guys to speak with me when you end up selling them. So, using the - ing form after terminar, instead of de and the to form, gives us this meaning of “end up”. So, if you wanted to say, I want you guys to speak with me when you end up selling them, so I want you guys to speak with me:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: When you end up selling them, so we will say when you finish and then we will actually use the - ing:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Quiero que hablen conmigo cuando terminen vendiéndolos. I want you guys to speak with me when you end up selling them. So, there is a difference here, depending on whether we use the to form or the - ing form, just as we saw with empezar or comenzar, there can be a difference between using the to form afterwards or the - ing form afterwards, and both of them need a preposition to join the to form, which might be a clue to you that there is a difference between using the to form and then the - ing form. If the to form is joining with a preposition, then maybe this particular verb takes the - ing form afterwards as well with a slightly different meaning. Empezar a hablar, to start to speak, to start speaking; empezar hablando, to actually begin something speaking, or, as we would probably say, to start out speaking; terminar de vender, to finish selling; terminar vendiendo, to end up selling. So, what we have aimed to teach here is the idea of the mood tense, the general feeling of the mood tense, and the kind of constructions in which it occurs, and, like I said, understanding the mood tense is about listening out for it, and listening out for how people around you use it. A great way to learn it is to chat online, for example, because maybe in real life, and almost certainly at the beginning, you won’t pick up those subtle changes in vowels that you get with the mood tense. But if you are chatting online you will notice quite clearly, “ah, there they used an, oh why?” a and not an e, or there they used an e and not an a And then you will look at the context of the sentence and work out why the mood tense was used and that really is the best way to internalize it.
